summary: McKenzie shares an SEO tip from WebsiteGrader suggesting that domain registrations expiring within a year may be penalized by search engines as potential spam sites. He extended his domain registration by two years and prepaid hosting, noting his Google rankings improved afterward. He honestly acknowledges he cannot prove causation and hasn't seen this tip corroborated by trusted SEO sources. He frames it as a low-risk experiment since the $8 cost would be spent eventually regardless.
key_points: Search engines may penalize domains with short remaining registration periods, viewing them as potential spam sites
Extending domain registration is a near-zero-risk SEO experiment since the cost is deferred payment you'd make anyway
McKenzie is careful to distinguish correlation from causation in his own results
The tip came from HubSpot's WebsiteGrader tool but wasn't corroborated by other trusted SEO sources
money_quotes: Correlation != causation and all that aside, it only cost me about $8 so maybe you want to try it too.
Google and other search engines like to see domains that have been registered for extended periods of time as this shows a committment to the domain name.
If it isn't true the most you are out is about $8 which you were going to pay eventually anyway, right?
key_insight: Low-cost, low-risk SEO experiments like extending domain registration are worth trying even without definitive proof they work, since the downside is negligible.
